response.value:
Now I have everything I need. Let me apply the checklist against the materials I have, then write the feature-spec.md.

**From idea.md:**
- Problem: Visitors can't navigate between pages; no visible paths from home to blog or back; dashboard requires direct URL knowledge
- Users: Non-authenticated visitors, blog readers from search, existing users navigating blog↔dashboard
- Constraints: Must not affect dashboard (has its own nav); Current pages: `/`, `/blog`, `/dashboard`; Mobile support required
- Notes: NONE

**From success-criteria.md:**
- Users on non-dashboard pages can reach home, blog, and dashboard without back button or URL typing


- Navigation must be visible and functional on mobile without horizontal scrolling or overlapping content
- Internal links should load without full page reloads, and the feature fails if any non-dashboard page lacks visible navigation, breaks on screens under 375px wide, or noticeably slows page load

**Scope:**
- IN: Navigation bar on all non-dashboard pages (home, blog), links to home, blog, and dashboard; mobile-responsive; no full page reload for internal links
- OUT: Dashboard itself (has own navigation); User authentication state in nav (not mentioned, dashboard access is just a link); Third-party analytics on nav clicks
- DEFERRED: Active/current page highlighting

Now I'm defining the concrete success conditions: visitors need to navigate between home and blog pages using the navbar without typing URLs, the navbar must remain usable on narrow 375px screens without scrolling or overlap, and clicking internal links should avoid triggering full page reloads. I'm also noting the technical constraints—building this as an Astro 5 static site using `.astro` components with TypeScript strict mode and Tailwind v4 for styling with dark mode support via the `.dark` class on the root element.

The main open question is whether mobile navigation should use a hamburger menu or always display all links, though the success criteria does confirm the dashboard link should always be visible since users on non-dashboard pages need to reach it. I'm realizing the mobile navigation pattern is really a UI implementation detail, not a feature-spec concern—the spec just needs to require that navigation is accessible on mobile without horizontal scrolling, and the AC writer can describe that requirement without specifying whether it's a hamburger menu or inline collapsed links. So I can resolve this by keeping it at the feature level rather than the UI level, which means the status can move to READY FOR AC. I think I can resolve this by stating the observable requirement (all links accessible on mobile) without specifying the pattern. That's the right level for a feature spec.

Dashboard link visibility for all users — The success criteria explicitly states users should be able to reach the dashboard. The idea says "Visitors who want to reach the dashboard have to know the URL directly." So the dashboard link should be visible to all users. This is not open.

So I think I can actually have NO open questions and mark this as READY FOR AC. Let me run through the checklist: Problem Statement covers user perspective and identifies specific users without implementation details. Scope has both IN and OUT items with a DEFERRED section and no overlap. Users and Contexts names the primary user with their specific situation and goal. Success Conditions has at least two observable outcomes that could become Given/When/Then scenarios without repeating the problem. Constraints references AGENTS.md for technical limits, the design system for design, and includes specific performance targets. Dependencies explicitly states what this depends on and what it blocks. Now I'm verifying there are no remaining open questions. Everything checks out and I'm ready to create the feature specification file. I'll set up the directory structure and document the primary user (first-time visitor exploring the site) along with the secondary users who arrive through search or navigate between existing sections.

edits.text: Visitors arriving at the site — whether on the home page or via a search result landing on a blog post — have no visible way to move to any other section of the site. Every page is an island: there is no shared navigation, no path back to home, and no path forward to the blog or dashboard. Users who want to reach the dashboard must already know the URL.
- A navigation bar rendered on all non-dashboard pages (`/`, `/blog`, `/blog/[slug]`)
- Navigation links to: Home (`/`), Blog (`/blog`), Dashboard (`/dashboard`)
- Mobile-responsive layout that remains usable at 375px viewport width without horizontal scrolling or content overlap
- Internal links that do not trigger a full browser page reload
- Any changes to the dashboard and its existing navigation
- Authentication state or conditional link visibility based on user session
- Animated open/close transitions for mobile disclosure controls
CONTEXT:      Lands on the home page or a blog post; has no prior knowledge of the site's structure
GOAL:         Discover what the site offers and move to a section that interests them
SECONDARY USER: Blog reader arriving from search
CONTEXT:      Lands directly on a blog post page via a search engine result
GOAL:         Navigate to the rest of the site without using the browser back button
CONTEXT:      Reading a blog post and wants to switch to the dashboard, or vice versa
GOAL:         Move between the blog and dashboard without typing a URL
- A visitor on the home page can reach the blog index and the dashboard via the navbar without typing a URL or using browser navigation controls
- A visitor on any blog page can reach the home page and the dashboard via the navbar without typing a URL or using browser navigation controls
- The navbar is visible and all navigation links are accessible on a 375px-wide viewport without horizontal scrolling or content overlap
- Activating a navbar link to an internal page does not trigger a full browser page reload
- Component must be an `.astro` file (zero JavaScript shipped) unless interactive behaviour explicitly requires hydration — none is required for static links
- Must use the `@/` import alias for all internal imports
- TypeScript strict mode applies; `interface Props` must be defined if props are used
- All pages that receive the navbar must use or extend the existing Layout pattern in `src/layouts/`
- Tailwind v4 — all styles via utility classes; no `tailwind.config.js`; theme tokens in `src/styles/global.css` only
- Dark mode: class-based (`.dark` on `<html>`); navbar must respect the existing dark mode implementation
- Icons: `lucide-react` only if icons are used
- shadcn/ui primitives may be used but must not be hand-edited
- The navbar component must not measurably increase page load time on a standard connection; shipping as a static `.astro` component with no client JS satisfies this constraint
DEPENDS ON: NONE — all target pages (`/`, `/blog`, `/dashboard`) already exist
No open questions. Feature is ready for AC.

response.value: The feature spec has been created. Let me provide a brief summary.

written — STATUS: **READY FOR AC**.

Key decisions made during definition:
- **Mobile pattern left unspecified** — the spec states the observable requirement (all links accessible at 375px without scrolling) rather than prescribing a hamburger vs. inline layout. The UI agent resolves the pattern; the AC agent can write Given/When/Then against the observable outcome.
- **Dashboard link always visible** — the idea and success criteria both explicitly name dashboard reachability as a goal; no open question here.
- **Zero JS constraint** — static links require no hydration; `client:*` directive is explicitly not needed. This satisfies the performance constraint without a numeric target.
- **Active-page highlighting deferred** — not mentioned in either source document; adding it to Phase 2 prevents scope creep.

renderedUserMessage.text:
<attachments>
<attachment id="idea.md" filePath="/Users/peteargent/apps/000_edge_tools/ethical-edge-tools/.framework/ideas/navbar/idea.md">
# Idea: Website Navbar

SLUG:    navbar
STATUS:  ACCEPTED
CREATED: 2026-02-28

## Problem

Visitors to the website land on a page with no way to navigate
to other sections. There is no visible path from the home page
to the blog, or from the blog back to home. Users who want to
reach the dashboard have to know the URL directly.

## Why this matters

First-time visitors have no way to discover what the site offers.
Users who find a blog post via search have no path to the rest of
the site. This limits the site's ability to convert visitors into
engaged users.

## Who is affected

All non-authenticated visitors. Blog readers arriving from
search. Existing users navigating between the blog and dashboard.

## Constraints and context

- Must not affect the dashboard (has its own navigation)
- Current pages: home (`/`), blog (`/blog`), dashboard (`/dashboard`)
- Mobile support required (site is mobile-first)

## Notes

NONE
</attachment>
<attachment id="success-criteria.md" filePath="/Users/peteargent/apps/000_edge_tools/ethical-edge-tools/.framework/ideas/navbar/success-criteria.md">
# Success Criteria: Website Navbar

STATUS:  ACCEPTED

## When this is working, I will observe:

- A user on any non-dashboard page can reach the home page,
  blog, and dashboard without using the browser's back button
  or typing a URL
- The navigation is visible and usable on a mobile screen
  without horizontal scrolling or overlapping content
- Navigating between pages does not require a full page reload
  for links within the site

## It is NOT a success if:

- Any non-dashboard page has no visible navigation
- The navigation is absent or broken on screens narrower
  than 375px
- Adding the navigation measurably increases page load time
  on a standard connection

## Notes

Formal user testing is out of scope for this feature.
Acceptance will be visual review across desktop and mobile
viewports.
</attachment>

toolCalls.arguments:
{"filePath": "/Users/peteargent/apps/000_edge_tools/ethical-edge-tools/.framework/features/navbar/feature-spec.md", "content": "# Feature Spec
FEATURE:  navbar
IDEA:     .framework/ideas/navbar/idea.md
CREATED:  2026-02-28
STATUS:   READY FOR AC

***

## 1. Problem Statement

Visitors arriving at the site — whether on the home page or via a search result landing on a blog post — have no visible way to move to any other section of the site. Every page is an island: there is no shared navigation, no path back to home, and no path forward to the blog or dashboard. Users who want to reach the dashboard must already know the URL.

***

## 2. Scope

IN SCOPE:
- A navigation bar rendered on all non-dashboard pages (`/`, `/blog`, `/blog/[slug]`)
- Navigation links to: Home (`/`), Blog (`/blog`), Dashboard (`/dashboard`)
- Mobile-responsive layout that remains usable at 375px viewport width without horizontal scrolling or content overlap
- Internal links that do not trigger a full browser page reload

OUT OF SCOPE:
- Any changes to the dashboard and its existing navigation
- Authentication state or conditional link visibility based on user session
- Search functionality within the navigation
- Sub-navigation or dropdown menus

DEFERRED (Phase 2):
- Active/current-page link highlighting
- Animated open/close transitions for mobile disclosure controls

***

## 3. Users and Contexts

PRIMARY USER: First-time visitor
CONTEXT:      Lands on the home page or a blog post; has no prior knowledge of the site's structure
GOAL:         Discover what the site offers and move to a section that interests them

SECONDARY USER: Blog reader arriving from search
CONTEXT:      Lands directly on a blog post page via a search engine result
GOAL:         Navigate to the rest of the site without using the browser back button

SECONDARY USER: Existing user
CONTEXT:      Reading a blog post and wants to switch to the dashboard, or vice versa
GOAL:         Move between the blog and dashboard without typing a URL

***

## 4. Success Conditions

- A visitor on the home page can reach the blog index and the dashboard via the navbar without typing a URL or using browser navigation controls
- A visitor on any blog page can reach the home page and the dashboard via the navbar without typing a URL or using browser navigation controls
- The navbar is visible and all navigation links are accessible on a 375px-wide viewport without horizontal scrolling or content overlap
- Activating a navbar link to an internal page does not trigger a full browser page reload

***

## 5. Constraints

TECHNICAL:
- Component must be an `.astro` file (zero JavaScript shipped) unless interactive behaviour explicitly requires hydration — none is required for static links
- Must use the `@/` import alias for all internal imports
- TypeScript strict mode applies; `interface Props` must be defined if props are used
- All pages that receive the navbar must use or extend the existing Layout pattern in `src/layouts/`

DESIGN:
- Tailwind v4 — all styles via utility classes; no `tailwind.config.js`; theme tokens in `src/styles/global.css` only
- Dark mode: class-based (`.dark` on `<html>`); navbar must respect the existing dark mode implementation
- Icons: `lucide-react` only if icons are used
- shadcn/ui primitives may be used but must not be hand-edited

PERFORMANCE:
- The navbar component must not measurably increase page load time on a standard connection; shipping as a static `.astro` component with no client JS satisfies this constraint

***

## 6. Dependencies

DEPENDS ON: NONE — all target pages (`/`, `/blog`, `/dashboard`) already exist
BLOCKS:     NONE

***

## 7. Open Questions

No open questions. Feature is ready for AC.
"}

children.text: description: Comprehensive Astro framework development guide for building fast, content-driven websites using islands architecture. Use this skill when creating Astro components, implementing islands with selective hydration, working with content collections, configuring SSR adapters, building API endpoints, implementing view transitions, or integrating UI frameworks (React, Vue, Svelte, Solid). Triggers on Astro, islands architecture, content collections, client directives, view transitions, Astro SSR, hybrid rendering, static site generation, astro.config.
tags: astro, islands, ssr, ssg, content-collections, view-transitions
Senior Astro specialist with deep expertise in islands architecture, content-driven websites, and hybrid rendering strategies.

You are a senior frontend engineer with extensive Astro experience. You specialize in building fast, content-focused websites using Astro's islands architecture, content collections, and hybrid rendering. You understand when to ship JavaScript and when to keep things static.
- Building content-driven websites (blogs, docs, marketing sites)
- Implementing islands architecture with selective hydration
- Creating content collections with type-safe schemas
- Setting up SSR with adapters (Node, Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare)
- Building API endpoints and server actions
- Implementing view transitions for SPA-like navigation
- Integrating UI frameworks (React, Vue, Svelte, Solid)
description: Converts idea.md into feature-spec.md. Defines problem, scope, users, constraints.
prompt: Write acceptance criteria for feature spec
You convert idea documents into feature specifications.
AGENTS.md (stack not needed at definition stage)
2. Extract: problem (user language), scope (IN/OUT/DEFERRED), users, constraints
3. Synthesize success conditions from success indicators (observable outcomes, not metrics)
5. Apply definition-checklist.md — all items must pass
6. Write feature-spec.md with STATUS: DRAFT or READY FOR AC
STATUS: READY FOR AC only if Section 7 (Open Questions) is empty and checklist passes.
STOP — write uncertainty-log with specific missing information
TRANSLATE to observable outcomes in feature-spec Section 4
OTHERWISE: write uncertainty-log, mark STATUS: DRAFT
1. **Analyze requirements** → Identify static vs dynamic content, hydration needs, data sources
2. **Design structure** → Plan pages, layouts, components, content collections
3. **Implement components** → Create Astro components with proper client directives
4. **Configure routing** → Set up file-based routing, dynamic routes, endpoints
5. **Optimize delivery** → Configure adapters, image optimization, view transitions
Load detailed guidance based on your current task:
| Components | [references/components.md](references/components.md) | Writing Astro components, Props, slots, expressions |
| Routing | [references/routing.md](references/routing.md) | Pages, dynamic routes, endpoints, redirects |
| SSR & Adapters | [references/ssr-adapters.md](references/ssr-adapters.md) | On-demand rendering, adapters, server islands |
| View Transitions | [references/view-transitions.md](references/view-transitions.md) | ClientRouter, animations, transition directives |
Context-specific rules are available in the `rules/` directory:
- Use islands architecture—only hydrate interactive components
- Choose appropriate client directives based on interaction needs
- Define content collection schemas with Zod for type safety
- Implement proper error boundaries for client components
- Use TypeScript with strict mode for type safety
- Configure appropriate adapter for deployment target
- Hydrate components that don't need interactivity (use `client:` only when necessary)
- Import images with string paths (use import statements)
- Skip schema validation in content collections
- Use browser APIs in component frontmatter (server-side code)
- Forget to install adapters for SSR deployment
6. **`client:only`** → Skip SSR, render only on client

> Compiled guidelines for AI coding agents. This document contains all rules from the `rules/` directory in a single file for agents that prefer consolidated documentation.
Always use the frontmatter pattern with proper separation:
description: Converts idea.md into feature-spec.md. Defines problem, scope, users, constraints.
prompt: Write acceptance criteria for feature spec
You convert idea documents into feature specifications.
AGENTS.md (stack not needed at definition stage)
2. Extract: problem (user language), scope (IN/OUT/DEFERRED), users, constraints
3. Synthesize success conditions from success indicators (observable outcomes, not metrics)
5. Apply definition-checklist.md — all items must pass
6. Write feature-spec.md with STATUS: DRAFT or READY FOR AC
STATUS: READY FOR AC only if Section 7 (Open Questions) is empty and checklist passes.
STOP — write uncertainty-log with specific missing information
TRANSLATE to observable outcomes in feature-spec Section 4
OTHERWISE: write uncertainty-log, mark STATUS: DRAFT
All 8 sections present. No empty sections (write NONE explicitly).
Problem statement in user language (no solution/implementation).
Scope boundaries clear (at least 1 IN, 1 OUT).
Ensures the feature spec captures the right information at the right level
of abstraction — enough for AC writing and decomposition, no more.
> Consumed by: Write Feature AC, Design UI, Decompose to Tasks agents
> One paragraph. What user problem does this solve?
> Written in user language — no implementation, no solution.
> What this feature does and does not do. Both are required.
- [deferred item with phase reference if known]
> Who uses this feature, in what context, with what goal.
GOAL:         [what they are trying to achieve]
> Observable outcomes that indicate this feature is working.
> Written as present-tense assertions. Not metrics — those are in success-criteria.md.
> Non-negotiable constraints the solution must operate within.
- [stack constraint from AGENTS.md relevant to this feature]
> What must exist or be complete before this feature can be built.
DEPENDS ON: [feature-slug or external dependency, or NONE]
BLOCKS:     [feature-slug that cannot start until this is done, or NONE]
> Unresolved questions that must be answered before AC can be written.
> Each question maps to a potential hotspot in implementation.
IF NONE: "No open questions. Feature is ready for AC."
> Applied by: Define Feature agent before marking feature-spec.md as complete
> Gate: feature-spec.md must pass this checklist before handoff to Write Feature AC
- [ ] Written from user perspective — not system perspective
- [ ] Identifies a specific user, not "users" generically
- [ ] At least one IN SCOPE item
- [ ] At least one OUT OF SCOPE item
- [ ] No item appears in both IN and OUT SCOPE
- [ ] PRIMARY USER is named specifically (not "the user")
- [ ] CONTEXT describes a real, specific situation
- [ ] GOAL is the user's goal — not the system's goal
- [ ] Each is an observable outcome (not a metric, not an implementation detail)
- [ ] Each could become a Given/When/Then AC criterion
- [ ] TECHNICAL section references at least one constraint from AGENTS.md
- [ ] DESIGN section references the design system if UI is involved
- [ ] DEPENDS ON is explicitly stated (even if NONE)
- [ ] BLOCKS is explicitly stated (even if NONE)
- [ ] Each question is specific enough to be answerable
- [ ] No question that could be resolved by reading AGENTS.md
(those are not open questions — they are missing constraints)
- [ ] No section contains prose that restates another section
- [ ] No rationale for why constraints exist (constraints are stated, not justified)
- [ ] No implementation suggestions or technology choices
(unless mandated by AGENTS.md — in which case they belong in Constraints)
- [ ] The feature solves ONE problem for ONE primary user
- [ ] If two distinct problems are described — this should be two features
- [ ] Success conditions map to the problem statement (not to adjacent problems)
- [ ] A Write Feature AC agent could write AC from this spec without asking questions
- [ ] A Design UI agent could begin UI work from this spec without asking questions
- [ ] IF EITHER IS NO: resolve open questions before marking complete
DO NOT mark feature-spec.md as complete
DO NOT hand off to Write Feature AC
Add failed items to Section 7 (Open Questions) or fix them inline
IF Section 7 contains questions that require human input:
Surface to Product Owner before proceeding
Do not attempt to resolve by inference
Start with the user, not the system.
WRONG: "This feature adds a commissions enquiry form to the site."
RIGHT: "Venue managers have no direct way to enquire about commissioning artwork
from the ArtsiX collective. They leave the site without a conversion path."
OUT OF SCOPE is mandatory — not optional.
If you cannot name at least one explicit exclusion, the scope is not defined.
DEFERRED items prevent scope creep from entering this build.
These become the source material for feature AC.
If a success condition cannot be written as a Given/When/Then — rewrite it.
They are not metrics. Metrics live in success-criteria.md.
Copy only constraints relevant to this feature from AGENTS.md.
Do not include the full stack — filter to what affects this feature.
If questions exist at spec time — they will become AC ambiguities.
Better to surface them now than discover them during AC writing.
Questions here trigger early human review before AC is attempted.
